items.q: Is it legal to copy software for a friend if you paid for it yourself?
items.correct: No - buying software gives you a licence to use it, not ownership of the work; copying it for others infringes copyright under the CDPA 1988.
items.misconception: Yes - once you have bought something you may share copies freely.
items.random_wrong: Yes - copying is legal as long as no money changes hands.
items.why: Ownership of a copy is confused with ownership of the rights. The licence defines what you may do, and personal purchase does not include redistribution.

items.q: Which law covers hacking, and which covers careless handling of personal data?
items.correct: Hacking is covered by the Computer Misuse Act 1990; how organisations handle personal data is governed by the Data Protection Act 2018.
items.misconception: Both are covered by the Data Protection Act, which bans hacking.
items.random_wrong: Both are covered by copyright law.
items.why: The two laws constantly swap in answers. Match offender to law: an intruder breaking in (CMA) versus an organisation mishandling data it legitimately holds (DPA).

items.q: Is it fine to put an old laptop in the household bin?
items.correct: No - e-waste contains hazardous materials such as lead and lithium batteries; devices should be reused, donated or taken to proper recycling facilities.
items.misconception: Yes - electronics break down harmlessly in landfill.
items.random_wrong: Yes - landfill sites remove the batteries automatically.
items.why: Out of sight, out of mind. Landfilled electronics leach toxins and waste recoverable metals - the reason separate WEEE-style collection exists.

items.q: Accessing someone else's computer account without permission breaks which law?
items.misconception: The Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The Computer Misuse Act 1990. “The Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 protects:
items.correct: Original works such as software, music and images from unauthorised copying
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Original works such as software, music and images from unauthorised copying. “Personal data held by companies” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these counts as personal data?
items.why: Correct answer: A customer's home address. “A company's office chair count” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is an environmental issue caused by computing?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Toxic e-waste from discarded devices. “Strong passwords” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act passed?
items.why: The real figure is 1988. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
